skill.name: ddd-context-mapping
skill.description: Map relationships between bounded contexts and define integration contracts using DDD context mapping patterns.
skill.summary: Define integration contracts between bounded contexts using Domain-Driven Design context mapping patterns.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged markdown backticks as shell commands and weak cryptography patterns. All findings are FALSE POSITIVES - the skill contains only documentation and reference material with no executable code, network calls, or filesystem operations. Safe for publication.

content.user_title: Map bounded context relationships with DDD patterns
content.value_statement: Domain-Driven Design integration becomes complex when multiple bounded contexts interact. This skill defines clear contracts and anti-corruption layers between contexts using proven DDD patterns.
actual_capabilities: Identify and map relationships between bounded contexts in your domain
Select appropriate context mapping patterns like Partnership, Customer-Supplier, or Conformist
Define anti-corruption layer boundaries to protect domain integrity
Create contract ownership matrices for clear team responsibilities
Document translation rules between different ubiquitous languages
Establish failure modes and versioning policies for context integrations
limitations: Does not replace detailed API schema design or technical contract specifications
Cannot guarantee organizational alignment without team buy-in and process changes
Requires periodic review when team ownership or business domains evolve
Focuses on strategic DDD patterns, not tactical implementation details

use_cases.title: Microservices Integration Planning
use_cases.description: Map how Checkout, Billing, Inventory, and Fraud contexts integrate before implementing service boundaries.

use_cases.title: Legacy System Migration
use_cases.description: Define anti-corruption layers when integrating new domains with existing monolithic systems.
use_cases.target_user: Engineering leads managing legacy modernization projects

use_cases.title: Cross-Team Contract Definition
use_cases.description: Clarify upstream and downstream ownership to prevent domain leakage and unclear responsibilities.
use_cases.target_user: Technical product managers coordinating multi-team initiatives

prompt_templates.title: Basic Context Mapping
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze my domain with these bounded contexts: [list contexts]. Identify the relationships between each pair and recommend appropriate context mapping patterns from DDD.
prompt_templates.scenario: Initial domain analysis for a new system

prompt_templates.title: Anti-Corruption Layer Design
prompt_templates.prompt: I need to integrate with [external system/context]. Design an anti-corruption layer that translates their model into my ubiquitous language while protecting my domain core.
prompt_templates.scenario: Third-party integration with different domain model

prompt_templates.title: Contract Ownership Matrix
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a contract ownership matrix for these context pairs: [list pairs]. Define who owns each contract, what translation is needed, and the coupling risk level.

prompt_templates.title: Failure Mode Analysis
prompt_templates.prompt: For the [upstream] to [downstream] context integration using [pattern], identify failure modes, define fallback behaviors, and establish a versioning policy.

output_examples.input: Map relationships between Checkout, Billing, and Inventory contexts for an e-commerce platform
output: Context Map: Checkout-Billing (Customer-Supplier, Billing owns contract)
Context Map: Checkout-Inventory (Partnership, shared contract ownership)
ACL required at Billing boundary to translate payment terms
Coupling risk: Inventory availability changes affect Checkout flow

output_examples.input: Design integration between new Order context and legacy ERP system
output: Pattern: Anti-Corruption Layer between Order and ERP
Order context defines canonical Order model
ACL translates ERP terminology to Order ubiquitous language
Contract tests validate ACL behavior for all ERP scenarios

best_practices: Keep anti-corruption layer code at domain boundaries, not inside domain core
Add contract tests to verify translated behavior matches expected semantics
Revisit context maps when team ownership or business domains change
anti_patterns: Allowing downstream contexts to depend directly on upstream internal models
Creating shared kernels between contexts that should remain independent
Skipping translation layers when integrating with external or legacy systems

faq.question: When should I use an Anti-Corruption Layer versus a Conformist pattern?
faq.answer: Use ACL when the upstream model conflicts with your ubiquitous language or when integrating with external systems. Use Conformist when the upstream model is stable and aligns with your needs.

faq.question: How many bounded contexts should a typical system have?
faq.answer: There is no fixed number. Contexts emerge from business domains and team boundaries. Start with major business capabilities and split when you detect conflicting ubiquitous languages.

faq.question: Can a context have multiple relationship patterns with another context?
faq.answer: Yes, different aggregates within contexts may use different patterns. Document each relationship separately to avoid confusion.

faq.question: What is a Shared Kernel and when should I avoid it?
faq.answer: Shared Kernel is a subset of the domain model shared by two teams. Avoid it when contexts need independent evolution or when team coordination overhead becomes too high.

faq.question: How do I handle versioning when context contracts change?
faq.answer: Establish a versioning policy upfront. Support backward compatibility during transition periods. Use contract tests to detect breaking changes before deployment.

faq.question: Does this skill work for monolithic applications?
faq.answer: Yes, bounded contexts can exist within monoliths as modular boundaries. Context mapping helps plan future service extraction and prevents domain leakage across modules.
